summary: Phase 5.2 splits the generic-agent flow into create (queue) and execute primitives, adds a run-scoped one-shot scheduler, aggregation generation, three new CLI commands, and strengthens package-smoke CI. The code is well-structured with strong input validation, path-traversal defenses (job_id segment check, artifact path containment, input payload cross-validation), and no state.json mutation in the scheduler/aggregation paths. Backward compatibility for run-generic-agent is preserved by delegation to create+execute. Test coverage is extensive (940+ new test lines covering create/execute/scheduler/aggregate/CLI paths and the Phase 5 live run artifact). The implementation explicitly does NOT implement multi-worker locking, watch mode, cloud queues, or stale-running recovery; these are documented as residual risks. The primary limitations are TOCTOU races inherent to single-process file-based claiming (acceptable for the declared scope) and minor CLI normalization edge cases. No critical or high-severity defects found within the declared scope.

findings.title: Job claim is not cross-process atomic (TOCTOU between status read and running write)
findings.evidence: execute_generic_agent_job reads job.json (status='queued'), validates input/artifacts, then sets job['status']='running' and writes job.json via write_json_file. There is no file lock, atomic rename swap, or compare-and-swap between the read and the write. Two concurrent scheduler processes could both observe 'queued' and both proceed to execute, producing duplicate output and corrupted artifacts. The plan explicitly states multi-worker locks are out of scope, but the task description says 'atomically claims queued jobs', which is only true within a single process.
findings.recommendation: Document the single-process concurrency model prominently in the CLI --help text and the design spec. If multi-worker support is ever needed, use an atomic claim primitive such as os.replace of a unique claim file or os.link (hard link) which fails if the target exists, before setting status to running.

findings.title: _normalize_queue_generic_agent_argv rejects option values that start with --
findings.evidence: In the space-separated option form, the normalizer checks: if index + 1 >= len(tail) or tail[index + 1].startswith('--'): parser.error(...). This means an agent name or adapter value that legitimately begins with '--' (e.g. --agent --weird-name) would be rejected as a missing argument. The equals form (--agent=--weird-name) works because the startswith(f'{option_name}=') branch handles it, but the space form is silently broken for such values.
findings.recommendation: Either document that values starting with -- must use the --key=value form, or relax the check to only reject tokens that exactly match a known option name (rather than any token starting with --). At minimum, add a test for the equals-form workaround of a --prefixed value to lock in the supported escape hatch.

findings.title: Error message change for invalid job_id could break callers matching on error text
findings.evidence: The old code raised 'job_id escapes jobs directory' for traversal attempts; the new validate_generic_agent_job_id raises 'job_id must be a single safe path segment'. The existing test was updated to assert the new string, but any external caller or log parser that matches on the old error text would silently miss the new error.
findings.recommendation: If error-text stability matters for downstream tooling, add the old phrasing to the new error or document the error vocabulary change in the spec. Otherwise, note the message change in the migration/progress notes so consumers are aware.

findings.title: remove_created_run_dir called with job_dir despite function name suggesting run_dir
findings.evidence: create_generic_agent_job's exception handler calls remove_created_run_dir(job_dir) to clean up on write failure. The function name and parameter name (run_dir: Path) suggest it was designed for run-directory cleanup, but it is now reused for job-directory cleanup. The test test_create_generic_agent_job_cleans_job_dir_when_input_write_fails confirms it removes the job_dir correctly, so the behavior is correct, but the naming is misleading for future maintainers.
findings.recommendation: Rename the helper to remove_created_dir(target: Path) or add a docstring noting it removes any created directory, to avoid confusion about whether it has run-specific cleanup logic (e.g., state.json checks) that could misfire on a job_dir.

findings.title: Aggregation generated_at is non-deterministic across runs
findings.evidence: aggregate_jobs sets aggregation['generated_at'] = utc_now(), so re-running aggregation on the same job artifacts always produces a different aggregation.json. This is expected for a generation timestamp, but it means the committed aggregation.json artifact is a point-in-time snapshot and cannot be byte-for-byte reproduced.
findings.recommendation: This is acceptable. If reproducible aggregation is ever needed (e.g., for golden-file testing of the committed artifact), consider adding an optional --generated-at override or documenting that committed aggregation.json is not reproducible.

findings.title: CI smoke test creates agent script via heredoc without HARNESS_AGENT_INPUT_FILE/OUTPUT_FILE env documentation
findings.evidence: The CI heredoc agent reads os.environ['HARNESS_AGENT_INPUT_FILE'] and writes os.environ['HARNESS_AGENT_OUTPUT_FILE']. These env vars are set by run_agent_subprocess (not shown in diff). The contract is implicitly tested by the package-smoke flow, but if the env var names change, the CI agent would fail with a KeyError rather than a clear validation error.
findings.recommendation: Consider documenting the HARNESS_AGENT_INPUT_FILE / HARNESS_AGENT_OUTPUT_FILE / HARNESS_AGENT_RAW_LOG_FILE environment contract in the CLI help or a short agent-authoring guide so smoke-test authors know the required env vars without reading the source.

tested: create_generic_agent_job writes queued artifacts (job.json/input.json) without executing, without mutating state.json, without creating raw.log
create_generic_agent_job validates job_id (non-string, empty, slash, backslash, dot, absolute), agent, adapter, command (non-list, non-string items) before creating job_dir
create_generic_agent_job cleans up job_dir on input write failure (mocked OSError)
execute_generic_agent_job consumes a pre-existing queued job, runs the agent, writes output/raw.log, sets terminal status, does not mutate state.json
execute_generic_agent_job rejects jobs with pre-existing output.json or raw.log before claiming (no overwrite)
execute_generic_agent_job rejects running and terminal jobs without overwriting raw.log
execute_generic_agent_job rejects non-string job_id, slash/backslash job_id before touching artifacts
execute_generic_agent_job rejects artifact paths (input_file/output_file/raw_log_file) that escape job_dir via ../../
execute_generic_agent_job rejects job_id/run_id mismatch between job.json and state.json
execute_generic_agent_job validates input payload shape (non-object, non-string command, identity field mismatches, path escape/mismatch) before claiming
scheduler_run_once executes queued jobs in created_at/job_id order and continues after a terminal failed job
scheduler_run_once skips running and terminal jobs without claiming them
scheduler_run_once aborts on schema-invalid or semantically-invalid job records before executing any queued job
aggregate_jobs classifies terminal jobs into succeeded/failed/timeout/cancelled buckets, lists incomplete jobs, collects findings and residual_risks, does not mutate state.json
aggregate_jobs records missing or invalid terminal output as residual_risk
aggregate_jobs aborts on schema-invalid or semantically-invalid job records without writing aggregation.json
run_generic_agent backward compatibility via create+execute delegation (existing tests unchanged)
CLI queue-generic-agent -> run-scheduler --once -> aggregate-jobs end-to-end via python -m harness.cli
CLI queue accepts space-separated and equals-style options (--agent=foo --adapter=bar --timeout-seconds=30) after job_id
CLI queue rejects missing --adapter and --timeout-seconds values without creating job_dir
CLI queue preserves agent-side options after the -- separator (e.g., --agent=inner-agent passed through to the command list)

Phase 5 live run artifact (harness/runs/2026-06-22-phase-5-live-scheduler-smoke) validates, has completed status, indexes agent-job/agent-result/aggregation evidence, raw.log contains expected output, aggregation consumed_jobs matches
CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) package-smoke covers queue -> scheduler --once -> aggregate-jobs -> index-evidence (agent-job, agent-result, aggregation) -> validate
Static contracts test references new CLI commands and CI smoke agent script name
Verification reports 253 unittest tests OK (skipped=1), all 12 source-controlled runs validate, git diff --check clean, non-editable package smoke passed
not_tested: Real GitHub Actions run for this branch (verification explicitly states not triggered yet)
Cross-process / multi-worker concurrency (two schedulers claiming the same queued job)
Automatic stale-running recovery (orphaned running jobs are skipped, not recovered)

Full implementation of remove_created_run_dir, same_path, is_within_path, run_agent_subprocess, validate_job_timestamp_semantics, validate_agent_result_matches_job (not in diff; assumed pre-existing and unchanged)
Windows-specific path separator and case-insensitivity behavior in Path.resolve / is_within_path / same_path
Behavior when jobs/ directory contains a very large number of job subdirectories (scheduler loads and validates all before executing any)
Behavior when agent command writes to output_file asynchronously or does not exit (timeout enforcement path not shown in diff)
Aggregation schema validation of the written aggregation.json (diff shows write but not a post-write schema validate of aggregation.json itself)
residual_risks: Single-process scheduler only: no file locking or CAS on job claim; concurrent invocations of run-scheduler --once or execute_generic_agent_job on the same run could double-execute the same queued job and corrupt artifacts.

No stale-running recovery: a job stuck in 'running' (e.g., process killed between status write and completion) will be skipped forever by the scheduler and listed as incomplete by aggregation, with no automatic retry or alerting.
CI workflow has not been exercised on a real GitHub Actions runner; the heredoc-generated agent script and the packaged console-script flow are only validated by local verification at this time.
Aggregation generated_at is non-reproducible; the committed aggregation.json is a point-in-time snapshot, not a deterministic function of job artifacts.
CLI queue-generic-agent space-separated options cannot accept values beginning with '--'; users must use the --key=value form for such values.

If load_scheduler_jobs encounters a job whose status is a valid enum value but semantically inconsistent with timestamps (e.g., 'succeeded' with null completed_at), the scheduler aborts ALL execution; a single bad job record blocks the entire queue. This is by design but could be operationally surprising.
